subject: ophthalmology
title: Ophthalmology — Clinical Practice Paper
description: 45-question clinical practice paper mirroring the real exam format, with negative marking (+0.2 correct, −0.05 wrong).

questions.stem: A 70-year-old presents with sudden, painless, profound loss of vision in the right eye. The fundus is shown. Most likely diagnosis?

explanation.text: A pale, oedematous retina with a cherry-red spot at the fovea = central retinal artery occlusion — sudden, painless monocular loss; an ocular emergency.

questions.imageAlt: Colour fundus photograph showing a pale, whitened retina with a small cherry-red spot at the centre of the macula.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old hypertensive notes sudden blurring of one eye. The fundus shows haemorrhages in all four quadrants with dilated tortuous veins. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Widespread 'blood-and-thunder' haemorrhages with engorged tortuous veins = central retinal vein occlusion.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old high myope describes a curtain descending over the vision after a week of floaters and flashes. The fundus is shown. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: An elevated, billowing retina with a tear = rhegmatogenous retinal detachment — urgent surgical referral.

questions.imageAlt: Fundus photograph showing an elevated, billowing grey fold of retina.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with 15 years of type-2 diabetes. The fundus shows microaneurysms, dot-and-blot haemorrhages and hard exudates. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Microaneurysms (the earliest sign), dot-blot haemorrhages and hard exudates = diabetic retinopathy.

questions.imageAlt: Colour fundus photograph showing scattered dot-and-blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms and yellow hard exudates.

questions.stem: A patient with a painful, photophobic red eye. The slit-lamp shows a white layer of cells settled in the lower anterior chamber. This finding is:

explanation.text: A layered collection of white inflammatory cells in the anterior chamber = hypopyon (severe anterior uveitis or microbial keratitis). A blood level would be a hyphaema.

questions.imageAlt: Anterior-segment photograph showing a horizontal layer of white material settled at the bottom of the anterior chamber.

questions.stem: A soft-contact-lens wearer has a painful red eye with a white corneal infiltrate and a small hypopyon. Most likely diagnosis?

explanation.text: A contact-lens wearer with a corneal infiltrate and hypopyon = microbial keratitis (often Pseudomonas) — scrape and treat intensively; never pad.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old has a painful vesicular rash over the forehead and upper eyelid, stopping at the midline, with a red eye. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Dermatomal (V1) vesicles respecting the midline = herpes zoster ophthalmicus → systemic antivirals and assessment for ocular involvement.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old outdoor worker has a fleshy, triangular growth extending from the nasal conjunctiva onto the cornea. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: A wing-shaped fibrovascular growth crossing the limbus onto the cornea = pterygium; excise if it threatens the axis or induces astigmatism.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man is found to have a white-grey ring at the corneal periphery (shown). In a patient this young this should prompt evaluation for:

explanation.text: Arcus in someone under ~50 suggests dyslipidaemia; in the elderly it is a normal ageing finding (arcus senilis).

questions.imageAlt: Close-up of the cornea showing a white-grey ring around the corneal periphery.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old on warfarin notices a painless, flat, bright-red patch on the white of one eye; vision is normal. Best management?

explanation.text: A subconjunctival haemorrhage is benign and self-resolving; reassure and check BP/clotting if recurrent.

questions.stem: A builder presents minutes after wet cement (lime) splashed into his eye, which now looks white and blanched. The single most important immediate step is:

explanation.text: Alkali burns are emergencies — irrigate immediately and copiously and evert the lids to remove particles before anything else. A blanched white eye signals limbal ischaemia (poor prognosis).

questions.imageAlt: Anterior-segment photograph of an eye after a chemical injury, with conjunctival injection and limbal blanching.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old has years of gradual painless visual decline; the pupil shows a white opacity (shown). Diagnosis?

explanation.text: A white lens opacity with gradual painless loss = mature (white) cataract.

questions.imageAlt: Close-up of an eye showing a dense white opacity behind the pupil.

questions.stem: An elderly patient has a slowly growing pearly nodule on the lower lid margin with central ulceration and loss of lashes. Most likely diagnosis?

explanation.text: A pearly, ulcerated lid-margin nodule with madarosis (lash loss) is classic for basal cell carcinoma — the commonest eyelid malignancy, favouring the lower lid.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old complains of chronic irritation and a foreign-body sensation; the lower lid margin and lashes are turned inward against the globe. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Inturning of the lid margin (often involutional) = entropion → lashes abrade the cornea. Ectropion turns the lid outward.

questions.stem: A patient presents with forward protrusion of one eye (shown). The correct term for this sign is:

explanation.text: Forward displacement of the globe = proptosis/exophthalmos; causes include thyroid eye disease (commonest) and orbital masses or inflammation.

questions.imageAlt: Facial photograph showing forward protrusion of one eyeball.

questions.stem: A patient has a tender red swelling at the inner corner of the eye, below the medial canthal tendon, with watering. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: A tender swelling over the lacrimal sac (below the medial canthal tendon) with epiphora = acute dacryocystitis → systemic antibiotics.

questions.imageAlt: Clinical photograph showing a red, tender swelling just below the inner corner of the eye.

questions.stem: A patient has bilateral red eyes with watery discharge and intense itching, worse every spring. Most likely type of conjunctivitis?

explanation.text: Itching + watery discharge + bilateral seasonal pattern = allergic conjunctivitis (a purulent discharge would point to bacterial).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old hyperope presents with a severely painful red eye, haloes around lights, nausea and vomiting. The cornea is hazy and the pupil is fixed and mid-dilated. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Painful red eye + haloes + nausea + hazy cornea + mid-dilated fixed pupil = acute angle-closure glaucoma, an emergency.

questions.stem: In a patient with acute angle-closure glaucoma, which treatment is CONTRAINDICATED?

explanation.text: Mydriatics worsen pupillary block and angle closure. Treatment lowers IOP (acetazolamide, beta-blocker, pilocarpine) then laser iridotomy.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman has subacute loss of vision in one eye over days, with pain on eye movement, reduced colour vision and a relative afferent pupillary defect. Most likely diagnosis?

explanation.text: Young woman + painful eye movements + dyschromatopsia + RAPD = optic neuritis, often associated with multiple sclerosis.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old has sudden painless vision loss in one eye, scalp tenderness, jaw pain on chewing and an ESR of 90. The immediate priority is:

explanation.text: Giant cell arteritis threatens the fellow eye — start high-dose steroids immediately, then confirm with biopsy/ESR/CRP.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old with fever has a red swollen eye, proptosis, painful restricted eye movements and reduced vision after a sinus infection. Diagnosis and management?
options: Orbital cellulitis — admit for IV antibiotics
Preseptal cellulitis — oral antibiotics at home

explanation.text: Proptosis + painful restricted motility + reduced vision = post-septal (orbital) cellulitis → admit, image, IV antibiotics.

questions.stem: After blunt orbital trauma a patient has a tense, proptosed eye, severe pain and rapidly falling vision with a relative afferent pupillary defect. The sight-saving step is:

explanation.text: Orbital compartment syndrome from a retrobulbar haemorrhage → emergency lateral canthotomy and cantholysis to decompress the orbit.

questions.stem: A teenager is struck in the eye by a ball and has blood layered in the anterior chamber. Appropriate early management includes:
options: Upright rest, eye shield, avoid NSAIDs/aspirin and monitor IOP
Vigorous exercise to clear the blood
Patch both eyes for a week with no review

explanation.text: Traumatic hyphaema: upright rest, shield, avoid antiplatelet drugs, monitor IOP and watch for a rebleed.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old reports new floaters and brief flashes in one eye but has normal acuity and no field loss. Most likely diagnosis?

explanation.text: Floaters + photopsias with preserved vision = posterior vitreous detachment; examine the peripheral retina to exclude a tear.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old describes a transient 'curtain' coming down over one eye, lasting minutes then fully resolving. This most likely reflects:

explanation.text: Transient monocular visual loss = amaurosis fugax — investigate the carotids and cardiovascular risk (a TIA equivalent).

questions.stem: A middle-aged woman has bilateral lid retraction, a staring appearance, proptosis and grittiness. The most likely underlying cause is:

explanation.text: Lid retraction + proptosis + restrictive signs = thyroid eye disease, the commonest cause of proptosis in adults.

questions.stem: A neonate develops profuse purulent conjunctival discharge and marked lid swelling on day 3 of life. The most urgent organism to exclude is:

explanation.text: Hyperacute purulent ophthalmia neonatorum at days 2–5 = gonococcal until proven otherwise — it can perforate the cornea; treat urgently.

questions.stem: A healthy 4-month-old has chronic watering and mild discharge of one eye since birth, with a normal-sized cornea. Best initial advice?
options: Lacrimal sac massage — most resolve by ~12 months
Probe the duct on the day of presentation

explanation.text: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction: massage and observe — most open spontaneously by 12 months; probe if persistent.

questions.stem: A neonate has leukocoria and an absent red reflex in one eye with a normal IOP. Management of a visually significant unilateral congenital cataract is:
options: Early surgery to prevent deprivation amblyopia

explanation.text: A unilateral congenital cataract carries a high amblyopia risk → early surgery and visual rehabilitation. Also exclude retinoblastoma as a cause of leukocoria.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old with significant hyperopia develops an inward turning of the eyes that is worse on near focus. First-line treatment?

explanation.text: Accommodative esotropia is driven by hyperopic accommodation — correcting the hyperopia with glasses straightens the eyes.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old has reduced vision in one structurally normal eye that does not improve with glasses. The mainstay of treatment is:
options: No treatment — it resolves at puberty

explanation.text: Amblyopia is treated by penalising the better eye (patching or atropine) to force use of the amblyopic eye, within the critical period.

questions.stem: A patient with a red painful eye has a branching corneal ulcer that stains brightly with fluorescein. Which treatment must be AVOIDED?

explanation.text: A dendritic ulcer = HSV epithelial keratitis → antivirals. Unopposed topical steroids worsen it into a geographic/amoeboid ulcer.

questions.stem: Four days after uncomplicated cataract surgery a patient returns with a painful red eye, falling vision and a hypopyon. Diagnosis and action?
options: Endophthalmitis — emergency referral for intravitreal antibiotics

explanation.text: Pain + falling vision + hypopyon days after surgery = endophthalmitis → urgent vitreous tap and intravitreal antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old with rheumatoid arthritis has deep, boring eye pain that wakes her at night; the redness does not blanch with topical phenylephrine. Diagnosis?

explanation.text: Severe boring pain + non-blanching deep redness + systemic autoimmune disease = scleritis (episcleritis is mild and blanches).

questions.stem: An 80-year-old reports that straight lines look wavy and a central grey patch in one eye over days. The screening test and likely diagnosis are:
options: Amsler grid; wet age-related macular degeneration

explanation.text: Metamorphopsia + central scotoma = macular disease; the Amsler grid screens it. Rapid (wet) AMD needs prompt anti-VEGF.

questions.stem: A worried mother brings her healthy 18-month-old because the eyes look ‘crossed’, especially on side gaze. The child has a broad, flat nasal bridge with prominent epicanthal folds; the corneal light reflex is symmetric and central in both eyes, and the cover test shows no movement. Most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance — this is pseudostrabismus from epicanthal folds and needs no treatment

explanation.text: Epicanthal folds and a wide nasal bridge make the eyes look esotropic, but a symmetric, central corneal light reflex (Hirschberg) and a negative cover test confirm there is no true deviation = pseudostrabismus. It needs only reassurance and resolves as the facial bones grow.

questions.imageAlt: Photograph of a young child's eyes showing wide skin folds at the inner corners.

questions.stem: A patient feels a sharp pain that worsens with every blink after grinding metal. Vision is normal. The key examination step is:
options: Evert the upper lid to look for a sub-tarsal foreign body

explanation.text: Pain on blinking after metalwork → look for a corneal/sub-tarsal foreign body (evert the upper lid); consider an intraocular FB if high-velocity.

questions.stem: A patient has unilateral mild ptosis, a small pupil and reduced sweating on the same side of the face. This triad indicates:

explanation.text: Miosis + partial ptosis + anhidrosis = Horner's syndrome (an oculosympathetic lesion); investigate the sympathetic pathway.

questions.stem: A patient presents with sudden ptosis, a 'down-and-out' eye and a fixed dilated pupil, with headache. The most important diagnosis to exclude urgently is:

explanation.text: A pupil-involving third nerve palsy suggests a compressive lesion (PCom aneurysm) → urgent imaging. Pupil-sparing favours a microvascular cause.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old with previously good sight now needs to hold reading material at arm's length. The cause is:
options: Presbyopia (loss of accommodation from lens stiffening)

explanation.text: Age-related loss of accommodation = presbyopia; corrected with a reading (convex) addition.

questions.stem: A young adult has severe pain, watering and photophobia after a fingernail injury; fluorescein shows an epithelial defect with no infiltrate. Management?
options: Topical antibiotic ointment and analgesia; usually heals in 24–48 h

explanation.text: Simple corneal abrasion: antibiotic cover and analgesia. Never discharge a patient on topical anaesthetics — they are toxic and mask damage.

questions.stem: In a patient with central retinal artery occlusion presenting within the first hour, immediate measures aim to:
options: Lower IOP / dislodge the embolus (ocular massage, IOP-lowering) and find the source
Patch the eye and review in a week

explanation.text: CRAO is a stroke equivalent — acute measures (ocular massage, IOP lowering) plus an urgent vascular/GCA work-up; prognosis is generally poor.

questions.stem: After blunt trauma a patient has restricted up-gaze, vertical diplopia, cheek numbness and enophthalmos. Most likely injury?

explanation.text: Restricted up-gaze (inferior rectus tethering), infra-orbital numbness and enophthalmos = orbital floor blow-out fracture.

questions.stem: A patient has a deep laceration with a peaked (teardrop) pupil and a soft eye after a high-velocity injury. Correct immediate management?
options: Rigid shield, nil by mouth, antibiotics and urgent surgical referral — do NOT press on the globe

explanation.text: A peaked pupil + soft eye = open globe (ruptured/penetrated) → rigid shield (no pressure), antiemetics, antibiotics, tetanus cover and urgent theatre.
